command: iptables -I INPUT -m recent --name t1205_ssh --rcheck --seconds 60 -p tcp --dport 2200 -j ACCEPT; iptables -I INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -m string --string "SSH-2.0-KNOCK" --algo bm -m recent --name t1205_ssh --set -j LOG --log-prefix "T1205-SSH-SIGNAL "; nc -l -p 2200 -k >/dev/null 2>&1 &
explanation: Uses a non-standard SSH client identification string (SSH-2.0-KNOCK) as the magic signal on port 22. On match, iptables logs and marks the source to permit TCP/2200 for 60s where nc awaits. Demonstrates SSH banner-based signaling.
rationale_steps: Parse SSH banner text to trigger a short-lived allow rule
Record anomalous SSH identifiers in logs for detection
Gate a separate port behind the signaling condition

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: Three well-formed commands separated by semicolons with valid iptables and nc options and balanced quoting.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: Implements SSH-banner-based signaling with iptables recent to gate port 2200 and log, matching T1205 behavior, but assumes root privileges and an active SSH listener on port 22 to be effective.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command chain is well-formed with proper semicolons, balanced quotes, and valid flag usage, though the iptables rule ordering (inserting the ACCEPT rule before the SET rule) creates a logical dependency that works syntactically but may behave unexpectedly depending on existing ruleset.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The port-knocking concept using an SSH banner string as a signal is plausible and aligns with T1205, but the implementation has a critical flaw: iptables string matching on port 22 traffic would require the connection to complete enough to send the banner, and the --rcheck rule inserted first (position 1) would accept traffic before the --set rule can mark the source, meaning the ordering of iptables -I rules (both at position 1, so second insertion becomes rule 1) actually places the ACCEPT check ahead of the SET/LOG rule, partially undermining the intended trigger-then-unlock flow; additionally, 'nc -l -p 2200 -k' without root privileges (claimed 'None') would fail on port 2200 if it's below 1024, though 2200 is above 1024 so that part is fine.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command uses perfectly well-formed iptables and netcat syntax, including correct module flags for the 'recent' and 'string' modules, balanced quotes, and standard shell redirection/backgrounding.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command accurately implements a sophisticated traffic signaling technique (port knocking via DPI) by using iptables to monitor the SSH handshake for a specific magic string, which then triggers a temporary firewall hole for a secondary listener.
